nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among rocks and boulders in the surge zone at relatively shallow depths; grazes biofilm and algae from rock surfaces

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks and boulders covering much of the tank footprint
multiple broken lines of sight to disperse aggression
no plants (incompatible with high pH and heavy territory use)
open water swimming corridor along the front

diet.notes: Strictly herbivorous — feed exclusively spirulina-based flakes, algae wafers, blanched spinach, zucchini, and other vegetable matter. High-protein foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, beef heart, tubifex) must be completely avoided; animal protein triggers Tanganyikan bloat, a frequently fatal internal infection that is the leading cause of death in captive Tropheus. Many experienced keepers feed exclusively spirulina flake and spirulina wafers. Even protein-containing flakes marketed as \"tropical\" are too rich. Gut-loaded vegetables and commercial Rift Lake herbivore foods are ideal.

goodWith: other Tropheus of the same locality color form (same geographic morph only — never mix forms)
large Petrochromis species (similar care, compatible aggression levels)
Julidochromis species in very large tanks with adequate territory
other robust Lake Tanganyika cichlids of comparable size
avoidWith: Tropheus of different locality color forms (hybridizes irreversibly — genetic contamination is permanent)
Tropheus duboisi unless keeping as a deliberate single-species display (even then, mix risks)
small or timid fish (will be harassed)
mbuna (Malawi cichlids — incompatible water parameters and aggression dynamics)
any fish fed a high-protein diet that contaminates the tank food environment

imageCandidates.notes: Tropheus moorii \"Bemba\" color form, lateral view showing orange band. Clear species shot.
FishBase reference image for Tropheus moorii. License terms unclear.

summary: A strictly herbivorous, highly aggressive Lake Tanganyika cichlid available in dozens of genetically distinct locality color forms (\"Ilangi\", \"Bemba\", \"Kasanga\", etc.) that must never be mixed. Advanced care: protein causes fatal bloat; large groups of 12+ of one form only are required.
careNotes:
Tropheus moorii is one of the most demanding freshwater cichlids kept in the hobby. It is endemic to Lake Tanganyika’s rocky shoreline and requires water chemistry that closely mirrors the lake: pH 8.0–9.0, high hardness, and temperatures of 24–27°C. The most important husbandry rule is strict herbivory: animal protein in any form triggers Tanganyikan bloat (internal bacterial infection, often Aeromonas or Pseudomonas), which is difficult to treat and kills quickly. Feed only spirulina flake, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables. Even many “tropical” flake foods are too protein-rich.

Tropheus exists in over 50 documented locality color forms (\"Ilangi\", \"Kasanga\", \"Bemba\", \"Chimba\", \"Kachese\", \"Murago\", \"Bulu Point\", and many more), each genetically distinct and geographically isolated around the lake’s shoreline. These forms interbreed freely in captivity and produce fertile hybrids, so mixing forms causes irreversible genetic contamination. Hobbyists must purchase only a single locality form and keep records of its origin.

Conspecific aggression is very high. Tropheus do best in groups of 12 or more of the same form; in small groups dominant fish can kill subordinates relentlessly. A 300 L (approximately 80 US gal) minimum with a 150 cm footprint is widely cited, with larger tanks preferred. Decor should consist of extensive rock piles covering the majority of the tank bottom to create numerous hiding spots and broken lines of sight. A sandy corridor along the front gives subordinates escape routes.

Related species: Tropheus duboisi (fw-fish-535) is a closely related sister species with distinct white-spotted juvenile coloration; the same rules regarding locality forms, diet, and aggression apply.
breedingNotes: Tropheus moorii is a maternal mouthbrooder. After spawning on a flat rock surface, the female collects the eggs and incubates them orally for approximately 4 weeks. Broods are small (5–15 fry) compared to other cichlids. Females hold throughout incubation, eating little; a well-fed, healthy female will complete the hold. Fry are released fully formed and large enough to take finely crushed spirulina flake immediately. In a colony setup, females typically spawn regularly and the colony self-regulates. Deliberately separating mouthbrooding females to a bare breeding tank improves fry survival. Males may harass brooding females; sufficient group size and rock cover reduce this risk. Difficulty is rated as difficult due to demanding water quality requirements, diet precision needed to maintain brood stock in peak health, and the logistics of managing locality form purity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tropheus moorii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (pH 8.0–9.0, temp 24–27°C, hardness 8–18 dH), adult size (to 14 cm), tank size (minimum 240 L but 400+ recommended for colony), native range, habitat, diet (strict herbivore, bloat warning), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 4 week hold, 5–15 fry), and locality form hybridisation warnings.

additional.name: FishBase — Tropheus moorii
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Tanganyika shoreline, Tanzania, Zambia, DRC, Burundi), adult size (to 14.5 cm TL), temperature range (23–27°C), depth range (1–15 m), diet classification (herbivore, aufwuchs grazer). Confirmed strictly rocky littoral habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Tropheus Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed strict herbivory requirement and Tanganyikan bloat risk from protein. Tank size guidance (75–100 US gal / ~280–380 L for colony), group size minimum 12–15, locality form mixing warning. Temperature 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C) cited.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Tropheus species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed locality color form diversity (50+ documented forms), genetic isolation around Lake Tanganyika shoreline, irreversible hybridisation in captivity, mouthbrooder details (maternal, 4 weeks, small broods), and aggression management via large groups. Noted Tropheus duboisi as sister species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Keeping Tropheus: Lake Tanganyika’s Most Challenging Cichlid
additional.notes: Specialist coverage of Tanganyikan bloat pathology (bacterial proliferation triggered by dietary protein), colony dynamics, spawning behavior, and locality form cataloguing including named variants Ilangi, Kasanga, Bemba, Chimba, Kachese, Murago, Bulu Point. Confirmed advanced careLevel classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs orally for approximately 4 weeks. Broods small (5–15 fry). Requires pristine water, strict herbivore diet to keep broodstock healthy, and a large colony to allow natural spawning dynamics. Fry take finely crushed spirulina flake from release.
